summary.category: Sources of Light and Straight-Line Travel
points: Luminous objects emit their own light (Sun, stars, bulbs, fireflies), while non-luminous objects like the Moon reflect light from other sources. Light always travels in straight lines in a uniform medium, as demonstrated by activities with holes and bent pipes.

Transparent materials allow light to pass almost completely; translucent materials allow light to pass partially; opaque materials block light entirely. A shadow is a dark patch formed when an opaque object blocks light, and it requires three components: a light source, an opaque object, and a screen.

summary.category: Reflection of Light and Mirror Images
points: Reflection is the change in direction of light when it strikes a shiny surface like a mirror. A plane mirror forms images that are erect, of the same size, laterally inverted (left-right reversed), and virtual (cannot be captured on a screen).

A pinhole camera forms an inverted, real image on a screen through a tiny hole, demonstrating light's straight-line propagation. Practical applications of mirrors include periscopes (for observation through obstacles using two mirrors) and kaleidoscopes (creating symmetrical patterns through multiple mirror reflections).
